content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

February 15, 1991


Linda Goldberg
649-8 Woodside Sierra
Sacramento, CA  95825

                                        Re:  Your Request for Advice
                                             Our File No. A-91-065

Dear Ms. Goldberg:

     This letter is in response to your request for advice and
confirms the advice provided to you in the course of our telephone
conversation of January 31, 1991, regarding your duties and
responsibilities under the provisions of the Political Reform Act
(the "Act").1

                            QUESTION

     Under the provisions of the Act, may you accept discounted or
complimentary accommodations from the hotel industry?

                            CONCLUSION

     Under the facts presented, the Act does not prohibit you from
accepting discounted or complimentary accommodations from the
hotel industry.

                            FACTS

     You are a designated employee under the conflict-of-interest
code of the Assembly Rules Committee.  From time to time you have
submitted information regarding hotels, "bed and breakfast"
establishments and inns throughout the United States and Canada to
Sandra Soule.  Ms. Soule is the publisher of a travel guide
entitled America's Wonderful Little Hotels and Inns.  You have
received no compensation for your contributions to this travel
guide.


     1  Government Code Sections 81000-91015.  All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.  Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000, et seq.  All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.


428 J Street, Suite 800 ● P.O. Box 807 ● Sacramento CA 95804-0807 ● (916) 322-5660

File No. A-91-065
Page No. 2


     You wish to continue contributing to this publication.  You
will receive no payments for your contributions.  However, from
time to time you may receive reimbursement for your expenses to
purchase and develop film in connection with your activities.  You
may also receive courtesy or discounted accommodations at certain
establishments that you wish to visit.  You seek our advice to
determine whether you may accept these courtesy or discounted
accommodations in California and outside of California in the
future.

                                ANALYSIS

     As amended effective January 1, 1991, the Act imposes certain
restrictions on payments received by designated employees of state
agencies.2  Whereas receipt of income from the pursuit of a bona
fide business, trade, or profession is not restricted, the Act
prohibits receipt of honoraria and limits gifts to $250 in each
calendar year from each single source.  (Sections 89502(c) and
89505(a).

     With respect to honoraria, Section 89503 states that:

          (a)  No member of a state board or commission,
     and no designated employee of a state agency, shall
     accept an honorarium from any source, if the member
     or employee would be required to report the receipt
     of income or gifts from that source on his or her
     statement of economic interest.  This section shall
     not apply to any part-time member of the governing
     board of any public institution of higher
     education, unless the member is also an elected
     official.

                                Section 89503(a).

     You have advised me that you would be required to report
receipt of income or gifts from providers of lodging under the
provisions of the conflict-of-interest code of the Assembly Rules
Committee. Therefore, if the discounted accommodations constitute
an honorarium, the prohibitions of Section 89503(a) would be
applicable to your facts.

     The Act defines an honorarium as follows:

          (b)  "Honorarium" means, except as provided in
     subdivision (c), any payment made in consideration
     for any speech given, article published, or
     attendance at any public or private conference,


2    The term "state agency" includes the legislature.  (Section
82049.)

File No. A-91-065
Page No. 3


convention, meeting, social event, meal, or like
gathering.

     (c) "Honorarium" does not include earned
income for personal services which are customarily
provided in connection with the individual's
business or practice of a bona fide business,
trade, or profession, such as teaching, practicing
law, medicine, insurance, real estate, banking, or
building contracting, unless the sole or
predominant activity of the business, trade, or
profession is making speeches. The commission
shall adopt regulations to implement this
subdivision.

                    Section 89502(b) and (c).

     When you arrive at your destination, you will not be making a
speech or attending a gathering. You will simply take photographs
and observe the premises with a view to reporting your findings to
the editor of a travel guide. You are not receiving discounted
accommodations in consideration of an article prepared on behalf
of the innkeepers. Rather you will submit a report and
photographs to the editor of a travel guide who will then include
the information as one of many items related to lodging in
California. Therefore, the courtesy or discounted accommodations
you may receive are not honoraria for purposes of the Act.

     The courtesy or discounted accommodations are not income from
a bona fide business, trade, or profession. You have advised me
that you do not treat your expenditures in connection with your
travel as a business deduction for tax purposes, you do not
receive compensation for your trust, and in general you do not
perceive your activities as a business. You travel periodically
and share your findings with parties who may have an interest in
the information you make available to them. However, when you
receive courtesy or discounted accommodations, you have received a
payment which is reportable under the provisions of the Act.

     Any courtesy or discounted accommodations you may receive are
gifts. Gift means:

          ... any payment to the extent that
          consideration of equal or greater value is not
          received and includes a rebate or discount in the
          price of anything of value unless the rebate or
          discount is made in the regular course of business
          to members of the public without regard to official
          status....

                    Section 82028(a)

     You will receive a discount in the price of the
accommodations. However, the provider of these accommodations

File No. A-91-065
Page No. 4


will not receive consideration from you in exchange for the
courtesy or discounted accommodations. You will simply spend some
time as a guest at the lodging, photograph the surroundings, and
submit your favorable or unfavorable observations to the editor of
a travel guide. Therefore, the value of the accommodations is a
gift to you from the provider of the accommodations.

Gifts are limited under the provisions of the Act as follows:

     (a) No member of a state board or commission,
and no designated employee of a state agency, shall
accept gifts with a total value of more than two
hundred fifty dollars ($250) in a calendar year
from any single source, if the member or employee
would be required to report the receipt of income
or gifts from that source on his or her statement
of economic interest. This paragraph shall not
apply to any part-time member of the governing
board of any public institution of higher
education, unless the member is also an elected
official.

                                   Section 89505(a).

Therefore, you may not receive gifts in excess of $250 from any
one source.

     For purposes of the Act, gifts constitute income. (Section
82030(a).) Therefore, the fair market value of the courtesy or
discounted accommodations is reportable as income on Commission
Form 730 Statements of Economic Interests if valued at $50 or
more. (Section 87207(1) and Regulation 18726, copy enclosed.)

     We trust this letter adequately responds to your inquiry.
Should you have any further questions regarding this matter, do
not hesitate to call me at (916) 322-5901.

                                   Sincerely,

                                   Scott Hallabrin
                                   Acting General Counsel

                                   By: [signature]
                                       Bianca M. Breeze
                                       Counsel, Legal Division

SH:BB:dg

Enclosures

cornplimentary accommodations from the hotel industry?

CONCLUSION: Under the facts presented, the Act does not prohibit you frorn
accepting discounted or complirnentary accommodations from the
hotel industry.
